setting: English countryside drawing room, daytime, refined and quiet

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: Pride and Prejudice.

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: My dear Mr. Bennet, have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: Mr. Bennet replied that he had not.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: But it is; for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it.

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: Mr. Bennet made no answer.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: Do you not want to know who has taken it?

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: YOU want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it.

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: This was invitation enough.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: Why, my dear, you must know —

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: Mrs. Long had told her:

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: Netherfield is taken by a young man of large fortune from the north of England; that he came down on Monday in a chaise and four to see the place, and was so much delighted with it, that he agreed with Mr. Morris immediately; that he is to take possession before Michaelmas, and some of his servants are to be in the house by the end of next week.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: What is his name?

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: Bingley.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: Is he married or single? Oh! Single, my dear, to be sure! A single man of large fortune; four or five thousand a year. What a fine thing for our girls!

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: How so? How can it affect them?

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: His wife replied:

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: My dear Mr. Bennet, how can you be so tiresome! You must know that I am thinking of his marrying one of them.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: Is that his design in settling here?

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: Design! Nonsense, how can you talk so! But it is very likely that he MAY fall in love with one of them, and therefore you must visit him as soon as he comes.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: I see no occasion for that. You and the girls may go, or you may send them by themselves, which perhaps will be still better, for as you are as handsome as any of them, Mr. Bingley may like you the best of the party.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: My dear, you flatter me. I certainly HAVE had my share of beauty, but I do not pretend to be anything extraordinary now. When a woman has five grown-up daughters, she ought to give over thinking of her own beauty.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: In such cases, a woman has not often much beauty to think of.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: But, my dear, you must indeed go and see Mr. Bingley when he comes into the neighbourhood.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: It is more than I engage for, I assure you.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: But consider your daughters. Only think what an establishment it would be for one of them. Sir William and Lady Lucas are determined to go, merely on that account, for in general, you know, they visit no newcomers. Indeed you must go, for it will be impossible for US to visit him if you do not.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: You are over-scrupulous, surely. I dare say Mr. Bingley will be very glad to see you; and I will send a few lines by you to assure him of my hearty consent to his marrying whichever he chooses of the girls; though I must throw in a good word for my little Lizzy.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: I desire you will do no such thing. Lizzy is not a bit better than the others; and I am sure she is not half so handsome as Jane, nor half so good-humoured as Lydia. But you are always giving HER the preference.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: They have none of them much to recommend them. They are all silly and ignorant like other girls; but Lizzy has something more of quickness than her sisters.

blocks.speaker: mrs bennet
blocks.line: Mr. Bennet, how CAN you abuse your own children in such a way? You take delight in vexing me. You have no compassion for my poor nerves.

blocks.speaker: mr bennet
blocks.line: You mistake me, my dear. I have a high respect for your nerves. They are my old friends. I have heard you mention them with consideration these last twenty years at least.

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: Mr. Bennet was so odd a mixture of quick parts, sarcastic humour, reserve, and caprice, that the experience of three-and-twenty years had been insufficient to make his wife understand his character.

blocks.speaker: pp_narrator
blocks.line: Her mind was less difficult to develop. She was a woman of mean understanding, little information, and uncertain temper. When she was discontented, she fancied herself nervous. The business of her life was to get her daughters married; its solace was visiting and news.
